1: ʾēl ʾĕlōhîm YHWH dibbēr — the Mighty One, God, the LORD, has spoken. The solemn triple invocation of the divine name — ʾēl (the generic name for deity, the powerful one) + ʾĕlōhîm (the generic plural divine name) + YHWH (the covenant personal name). This judicial formula (cf. Joshua 22:22: ʾēl ʾĕlōhîm YHWH) convenes the divine court. Psalm 50 is the first of the Asaphite psalms (50, 73-83). wayyiqrāʾ ʾāreṣ mimmizerāḥ-shemesh ʿad-mĕbōʾô — and summons the earth from east to west. The divine summons of the entire earth as witness to the covenant lawsuit (rîb) — from sunrise to sunset, the universal scope of the judicial proceeding.
2: mîṣiyyôn miklal-yōphî ʾĕlōhîm hôphîaʿ — out of Zion, perfect in beauty, God has shone forth. Miklal-yōphî (perfection of beauty — the superlative of Zion's beauty; cf. Lamentations 2:15: ʾăsher ʾāmĕrû kĕlîlat yōphî of fallen Jerusalem). Hôphîaʿ (hiphil of yāphaʿ: to shine forth — the blazing appearance of the divine judge from Zion; the same verb as Psalms 94:1 where YHWH is asked to shine forth in judgment).
3: yābôʾ ʾĕlōhênû wĕʾal-yeḥĕrash — our God comes and will not stay silent. Ḥāresh (to keep silent, to hold one's peace — the accused calling on the judge to be silent; here YHWH will not remain silent). ʾēsh lĕphānāyw tōʾkhēl — fire devours before him. Fire as the divine judicial presence (cf. Exodus 3:2; Psalms 19:18; Deuteronomy 4:24; Psalms 97:3). ûsĕbîbāyw niśʿărāh mĕʾōd — and a fierce storm rages around him. Niśʿar (niphal: to be violently stormy — the storm-theophany surrounding the divine judge). The God who comes is neither neutral nor passive — fire and storm attend his arrival.
4: yiqrāʾ ʾel-hashshāmayim mēʿāl wĕʾel-hāʾāreṣ — he calls out to the heavens above and to the earth. The witnesses summoned: heaven and earth — the two parties in the cosmic covenant (cf. Deuteronomy 30:19: hāʿîdōtî bākem hayyôm ʾet-hashshāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ: I call heaven and earth to witness against you). lādîn ʿammô — to judge his people. Dîn (to judge — to enter into judicial proceedings against). The covenant lawsuit: YHWH is prosecuting Israel, not an external enemy.
5: ʾispû-lî ḥăsîday — bring together my faithful people. Ḥāsîd (the faithful, covenant-loyal ones — those who embody ḥesed). kōrĕtê bĕrîtî ʿălê-zābaḥ — those who sealed covenant with me by sacrifice. Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant — the standard covenant formula; covenants were ratified by cutting animals in two, as in Genesis 15:10-18; Jeremiah 34:18-20). The faithful are defined by their covenantal relationship — they sealed the covenant with sacrifice. Now the sacrifices are the subject of the lawsuit.
6: wayyaggîdû shāmayim ṣidqô — the heavens declare his righteousness. The heavens as witnesses now become witnesses to the divine righteousness — the cosmic court validates YHWH's justice. kî-ʾĕlōhîm shōphēṭ hûʾ selāh — for God himself is the judge. The selah after this declaration — God is the judge, both plaintiff and judge in the covenant lawsuit. The pause lets the weight of that identity settle before the divine speech begins.
7: shimĕʿāh ʿammî waʾădabbērāh — listen, my people, and I will speak. The divine speech begins — YHWH addressing ʿammî (my people: the covenant relationship intact even in the lawsuit). yisrāʾēl wĕʾāʿîdāh bākh — I will testify against you, O Israel. ʿûd (hiphil: to testify solemnly, to warn — the judicial testimony function). ʾĕlōhîm ʾĕlōhêkhā ʾānōkhî — I am God, your God. The self-identification formula — exactly the form of the Ten Commandments' preface (ʾānōkhî YHWH ʾĕlōhêkhā, Exodus 20:2). The covenant relationship is the basis for the lawsuit, not its repudiation.
8: lōʾ ʿal-zĕbāḥêkhā ʾôkhîḥekā — my rebuke is not over your sacrifices. The lawsuit is not a critique of offering sacrifices per se — they have been brought (wĕʿôlōtekhā lĕnegdî tāmîd: your burnt offerings are before me continually). The complaint is not the absence of sacrifice but the misunderstanding of its purpose.
9: lōʾ-ʾeqqaḥ mibbêtekhā phār — I will not take from your household a bull. The divine refusal of literal animal sacrifice as if God needed the animals — phār (bull — the most valuable sacrificial animal). mimdibĕrōtekhā ʿattûdîm — or goats from your pens. Dibrāh (fold, flock-enclosure) + ʿattûd (male goat — the leader of the flock). Both the largest and the most valuable animals are declared unnecessary as gifts to God.
10: kî-lî khol-ḥaytô-yāʿar — for every animal of the forest belongs to me. The ownership argument: YHWH already owns all the animals (as creator and sovereign), so the gift of an animal adds nothing to his holdings. behēmôt bĕʾalpê hārîm — the cattle on a thousand hills. The totality of domesticated and wild animals — all belong to YHWH. The sacrificial system cannot transfer to God what he already possesses.
11: yādaʿtî kol-ʿôph hārîm — I know every bird in the hills. Yādaʿ (to know — comprehensive divine knowledge of every creature). wĕzîz śādeh ʿimmādî — everything that moves in the open fields is mine. Zîz (moving thing, wild creature — possibly insects or small animals). All of creation's mobile life is with YHWH (ʿimmādî: with me). Nothing can be given to God as a gift since nothing is not-God's to begin with.
12: ʾim-ʾerʿab lōʾ-ʾōmar lākh — if I were hungry, I would not tell you. The absurd hypothetical pressed to make the point: if hunger were even possible for YHWH, he would not need to ask the worshiper — he could supply himself. kî-lî tēbēl ûmĕlōʾāh — for the world and all it contains is mine. Tēbēl ûmĕlōʾāh (the inhabited world and its fullness — the creation-claim formula of Psalms 24:1). What the worshiper offers comes from what already belongs to YHWH — the gift returns to its source.
13: hăʾōkhal bĕśar ʾabbîrîm — do I eat bull meat? The anthropomorphic assumption corrected: God does not eat the flesh of the sacrifices. wĕdam ʿattûdîm ʾeshteh — do I drink the blood of goats? The blood, which was specifically prohibited for Israelite consumption (Leviticus 17:10-14), is also not consumed by YHWH. The physical substance of animal sacrifice accomplishes nothing by transfer of matter to the divine.
14: zebah lĕʾĕlōhîm tôdāh — bring God a thank-offering as your sacrifice. The positive instruction — the alternative to misunderstood animal sacrifice. Tôdāh (thanksgiving, the thank-offering — from yādāh: to acknowledge/praise; the specific sacrificial genre in which the worshiper gives thanks for a completed divine rescue). wĕshallēm lĕʿelyôn nĕdārêkhā — and keep your vows to the Most High. Shālem nĕdārim (to fulfill/complete vows) — the ethical dimension: the sacrifice YHWH wants is keeping promises. ʿElyôn (the Most High) in the place of ʾĕlōhîm — the divine sovereign whose vows cannot be ignored.
15: ûqrāʾēnî bĕyôm ṣārāh — call on me when trouble comes. The covenant prayer-promise: the sacrifice God accepts is the prayer of the distressed person. ʾăḥalleṣkā wattĕkhabbĕdēnî — I will rescue you, and you will honor me. The reciprocal: divine rescue produces human honor of God. The true sacrificial cycle: prayer-rescue-praise, not animal-offering-divine satisfaction.
16: wĕlārāshāʿ ʾāmar ʾĕlōhîm — but to the wicked God says. The second part of the lawsuit turns from the misunderstanding of sacrifice (vv8-15) to the misuse of covenant language by those whose lives contradict it. māh-lĕkhā lĕsappēr ḥuqqāy — what right do you have to quote my laws? Sāphar ḥuqqay (to recount/recite my statutes) — those who recite the covenant law but do not keep it. wattisśāʾ bĕrîtî ʿălê-phîkhā — or to take my covenant on your lips? Nāśāʾ ʿălê-pheh (to take on the lips — to verbalize covenant language without moral correspondence).
17: wĕʾattāh śānēʾtā mûsār — you despise correction. Mûsār (discipline, instruction, correction — the educative action of God that the wicked reject). wattashlikhēhû dĕbāray ʾaḥărêkhā — and flung my words aside. Shalakh ʾaḥărāyw (to throw behind oneself — to dismiss contemptuously). The covenant words of God treated as unwanted refuse thrown over the shoulder.
18: ʾim-rāʾîtā gannāb wattirṣ ʿimmô — when you see a thief you go along with him. Rāṣāh ʿimmô (to be pleased with him, to approve — active complicity with theft). wĕʿim-mĕnāʾăphîm ḥelqĕkhā — and you make yourself at home with adulterers. The explicit listing of covenant-violations: complicity in theft and adultery (the seventh and eighth commandments reversed).
19: phîkhā shālaḥtā bĕrāʿāh — you let your mouth run loose into evil. Shālaḥ pheh bĕ (to send/let loose the mouth into) — the unrestrained mouth directed into evil speech. ûleshônĕkhā taṣmîd mirmmāh — and your tongue is busy weaving lies. Ṣāmad (to attach, join closely — the tongue joined to deceit as a craftsman works at a loom). Mirmāh (deceit, treachery — the crafted lie).
20: tēshēb bĕʾāḥîkhā tĕdabbēr — you sit and talk against your own brother. Yāshab + dibber bĕ (to sit and speak against — the posture of settled slander, deliberate and comfortable). bĕben-ʾimmĕkhā titten dōphî — you slander your mother's son. Ben-ʾēm (son of your mother — the closest familial bond, the full sibling) is the one slandered (dōphî: blemish, slander). The covenant violations cut through the most intimate human bonds.
21: ʾēlleh ʿāśîtā wĕhehĕrashttî — you did all these things, and I said nothing. The divine silence interpreted by the wicked as divine approval — the dangerous theological mistake. dimmîtā heyôt-ʾehyeh kāmôkhā — you concluded I must be just like you. Dimmāh (to think, imagine — to picture in the mind) + heyôt-ʾehyeh kāmôkhā (that I would be like you). The theological diagnosis: anthropomorphism in the deepest sense — projecting human moral character onto God, concluding that the silent God shares the worshiper's values. ʾôkhîḥăkhā wĕʾerkhāh lĕʿênêkhā — but now I rebuke you and lay everything out before your eyes. The divine silence is broken — the judicial speech (v7) delivers its full rebuke.
22: bînû-nāʾ zōʾt shōkĕḥê ʾĕlōhîm — take this seriously, you who have forgotten God. Shākhah ʾĕlōhîm (to forget God — the covenant failure of Deuteronomy 6:12; Psalms 8:11-14). pen-ʾeṭrōph wĕʾên maṣṣîl — or I will tear you apart, and no one will rescue you. Tāraph (to tear as a lion tears prey — the predatory divine judgment on those who forget God). The urgency: no rescuer will be available once the divine tearing begins.
23: zōbēaḥ tôdāh yĕkhabbĕdānnî — whoever brings thanksgiving as their sacrifice glorifies me. The closing positive statement (cf. v14): the tôdāh-sacrifice honors God precisely because it acknowledges dependence rather than attempting to obligate God through material gifts. wĕśām dārekh ʾarʾennû bĕyeshaʿ ʾĕlōhîm — to the one who walks a right path I will show the salvation of God. Śām dārekh (one who orders/prepares the way — who walks in the prepared path of the covenant) receives the divine salvation (yeshaʿ ʾĕlōhîm) as its natural outcome. The lawsuit closes not with condemnation but with the open door of thanksgiving and right-path-walking.
